content.text: Every tool call begins with a schema — a structured declaration that tells the model what tools exist, when to use them, and how to format their inputs. Writing clear, precise schemas is the single biggest lever for reliable tool use: a vague schema causes mistaken selections; a well-structured schema lets the model call the right tool with correctly typed arguments on the first try.

attrs.title: The four fields that make up an Anthropic tool definition

content.text: A tool schema is not just code documentation.
It is the model’s only source of truth about what each tool does and how to invoke it. The description field is read by the model at inference time to decide whether and how to call the tool.
Anthropic’s tool schema follows a specific structure passed in the
The four top-level fields serve distinct purposes:
Identifier used in tool_use response blocks. Must be unique across all tools.
Natural-language guide for when and why the model should call this tool.
JSON Schema object. Describes every parameter the model can provide.
Optional prompt-caching directive (not part of standard schema).
field uses standard JSON Schema. Anthropic supports the most common JSON Schema keywords:
string, integer, number, boolean, array, object
Passed verbatim to the model; be specific
Restricts to an explicit set of values
Model may use if user does not specify
Parameters the model must always provide
A single API call can declare many tools. The model chooses zero or more tools per turn based on what the user’s message requires.
Complex tools — those that accept structured data or variable-length lists — use nested schemas. Nesting is fully supported:
array types require an items sub-schema that defines element type
object types within properties require their own properties and required fields
Nesting depth beyond 2–3 levels makes schemas harder for the model to follow — prefer flat structures when possible
The model populates nested objects as plain JSON; ensure your handler performs the appropriate type casting
Once the model returns a tool call, your application must route the call to the correct handler and feed the result back as a
field in each result ties the result to the matching
block in an assistant message is followed by exactly one
block in the next user message.
Names must be unique within the tools array and are used verbatim in
blocks. Follow a consistent verb-noun pattern:

# GOOD — clear, verb-noun, unambiguous
"name": "search_products"
"name": "create_order"
"name": "cancel_subscription"

# BAD — generic, collides with other tools
"name": "search"
"name": "do_thing"
"name": "process"
The description is the model’s decision signal. It must answer:

# GOOD description — tells the model WHEN and WHY
"description": (
    "Search the product catalogue for items matching a query. "
    "Use this when the user wants to find, browse, or compare products. "
    "Returns up to 20 results with name, price, and availability."
)

# BAD description — states only what, not when
"description": "Searches products."
State what the tool returns, not only what it accepts
Mention disambiguation cues when multiple tools serve similar goals
Keep descriptions under 200 words; lengthy prose is not more helpful
guides the model in formulating the argument. Be specific about format, units, and constraints:
Include units: "Temperature in degrees Celsius (-50 to 60)"
Explain defaults: "Default to 'medium' if the user does not specify priority"
List common values in the description even when enum is not used
Add a description for every property
Model confuses tools; picks wrong one
Model fails when user omits the param
Use object or array schema instead
Model picks wrong tool between similar ones
parameter lets you override the model's default behaviour of selecting tools autonomously. Three modes are available:
— model decides; it may call zero or more tools (default).
— force a specific tool to be called on this turn.
— tool use is disabled; model must respond in prose.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Force the model to call a specific tool
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "tool", "name": "search_documents"},
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Tell me about quarterly earnings"}]
)
# stop_reason will always be "tool_use", block.name will be "search_documents"

# Allow auto-selection (default behaviour)
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "auto"},        # model decides whether to call a tool
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the capital of France?"}]
)
# stop_reason may be "end_turn" (no tool needed) or "tool_use"

# Disable tool use entirely for this turn
response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
    max_tokens=1024,
    tools=tools,
    tool_choice={"type": "none"},        # model must answer without tools
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise the previous results"}]
)

) is useful when you need to guarantee a structured output — for example, making the model always produce a JSON object by calling a schema-only tool. None mode is useful at the start of a conversation or when the model has enough context to answer without external data.
The model is generally good at following schemas, but it can occasionally produce incorrect types or omit required fields under unusual conditions. Validating
against the schema before executing the handler prevents runtime errors and gives Claude a chance to self-correct:
Returning a descriptive error string as the
content (rather than raising an exception) keeps the agent loop running — Claude reads the validation error, self-corrects its arguments, and retries on the next turn. Install
When a user requests data for multiple items, a batch tool schema avoids the round-trip overhead of calling a single-item tool many times. Use
constraints to guide the model on how to group calls:

Batch schemas reduce latency for multi-item lookups, but they shift validation complexity to the handler: your function must gracefully handle partial failures (some IDs valid, some not) and return structured results that let Claude report them clearly.
Tool schemas are the contract between your application and the model. Each schema has a
(parameter definition in JSON Schema). Good schemas use specific descriptions that state when to use the tool and what it returns, define parameter types and constraints precisely, and limit
to truly mandatory fields. With a well-formed schema, the model selects the correct tool and produces correctly typed arguments; the application dispatches the call and feeds the result back as a
